questions.question: Prove that DFS on a graph with n vertices and m edges has time complexity O(n + m) for adjacency list representation.
keyPoints: Each vertex is visited exactly once → O(n) operations
Each adjacency list is traversed once, examining each edge → O(m) operations
Total = O(n + m) = O(V + E)
For adjacency matrix: O(V²) as each vertex checks all possible neighbors

questions.question: Explain the four types of edges in DFS with examples. In which type of graph does each occur?
keyPoints: Tree edge: to unvisited vertex; exists in all graphs
Back edge: to ancestor (GRAY); indicates cycle in directed graphs
Forward edge: to descendant (BLACK via tree edge); only in directed
Cross edge: neither ancestor nor descendant; only in directed
Undirected graphs only have tree and back edges (others appear as back)

questions.question: State and prove the Parenthesis Theorem of DFS. How is it used in edge classification?
keyPoints: Theorem: For vertices u,v, intervals [d[u],f[u]] and [d[v],f[v]] are either disjoint or one contains the other
If u is ancestor of v: d[u] < d[v] < f[v] < f[u] → interval of v contained in u
If neither is ancestor: intervals are disjoint
Used for edge classification: v is descendant of u iff [d[v],f[v]] ⊂ [d[u],f[u]]

questions.question: How does DFS detect cycles in directed and undirected graphs? Explain with algorithm logic.
keyPoints: Directed: Cycle iff back edge found (edge to GRAY vertex)
Undirected: Cycle if edge to visited non-parent vertex
Use vertex colors: WHITE=unvisited, GRAY=in progress, BLACK=finished
When exploring u, if neighbor v is GRAY → back edge → cycle exists

questions.question: Explain Kosaraju's algorithm for finding Strongly Connected Components using DFS.
keyPoints: Step 1: Run DFS on G, store vertices by decreasing finish time
Step 2: Compute transpose graph Gᵀ (reverse all edges)
Step 3: Process vertices in order from step 1, running DFS on Gᵀ
Each DFS tree in step 3 is one SCC
Time complexity: O(V + E)

questions.question: Derive topological ordering of a DAG using DFS. Prove why reverse finish time gives valid order.
keyPoints: In DAG, for edge (u,v), vertex v discovered after u but finishes before u
Therefore f[u] > f[v] for all edges
Reverse order of finish times ensures for any edge (u,v), u appears before v
Algorithm: Run DFS, record finish times, output vertices in decreasing f[] order

questions.question: Compare recursive and iterative DFS implementations. When would you choose one over the other?
keyPoints: Recursive: uses call stack, cleaner code, risk of stack overflow for V > 10⁴
Iterative: uses explicit stack, no overflow, slightly complex code handling
Both have same time O(V+E) and space O(V) excluding stack
Choose recursive for simplicity when graph depth guaranteed < system stack limit
Choose iterative for large graphs or embedded systems with limited stack

questions.question: Let G be a directed graph with vertices {1,2,3,4,5} and edges: (1,2), (2,3), (3,1), (2,4), (4,5). Perform DFS starting from vertex 1 with adjacency list ordered by increasing vertex number. Give: (i) Discovery and finish times (ii) Edge classification (iii) Is there a cycle? Justify using DFS properties.
keyPoints: DFS from 1: d[1]=1, discover 2→3→1 (back edge!)
Edges: Tree: (1,2), (2,3), (2,4), (4,5). Back: (3,1) forms cycle
Cycle exists because back edge (3,1) found—vertex 1 is GRAY when exploring from 3
Classification: Tree edges form path 1→2→3 then branch to 4→5
Finish times help verify cycle: 1's interval [1,6] contains 3's [3,4] as 3 is descendant—edge to ancestor is back edge

questions.question: Prove the correctness of DFS: Show that DFS visits all and only vertices reachable from the source vertex. Use the White-Path Theorem.
keyPoints: Theorem: All vertices reachable from source s are visited

Proof by induction on discovery order: Base s visited initially. Inductive: assume all vertices at distance k visited. For vertex v at distance k+1, path s→...→u→v where u visited. When u processed, v is WHITE (unvisited) and path from u to v is all WHITE. By White-Path Theorem, v becomes descendant of u and is discovered. Thus all reachable visited
Only reachable visited: Vertex discovered only via tree edge from already-visited vertex, so must be reachable
Thus DFS visits exactly the reachable set

questions.question: Design an algorithm to find all articulation points in an undirected graph using DFS. Explain with an example graph.
keyPoints: Articulation point: removal disconnects graph
Key insight: Vertex u is articulation point if it has child v such that no back edge from v's subtree to ancestor of u
Use discovery times and low values: low[u] = min{d[u], d[w] for all back edges (u,w), low[child] for tree edges
Root is articulation iff it has ≥2 children in DFS tree
Non-root u is articulation iff it has child v with low[v] ≥ d[u]
Algorithm: DFS compute d[] and low[], then check articulation conditions
Example: In graph with vertices A-B-C-D where A-B, B-C, B-D: B is articulation point because removal disconnects C and D
